topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, nauseating knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and there it was: a notification confirming that my piece, the one I’d poured weeks of sleepless nights into, had been published under the name of some complete stranger. A cold, sharp sensation shot through my chest, followed by a slow, building heat that made my palms sweat. I reread the attribution, the unfamiliar name mocking me from the screen. I stood up so abruptly my chair scraped loudly across the hardwood floor. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I paced the small apartment, my footsteps sharp and uneven, each stride fueled by a rising tide of sheer, unadulterated fury. I wanted to scream, to shatter something, anything, but the sound caught in my throat, replaced only by a low, vibrating hum of intense frustration.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in ragged gasps. The words swam before my eyes, a grotesque parody of my own effort. It was published. Under *his* name. A slow, simmering resentment began to bubble up, starting in my gut and spreading outward, making my skin feel tight and prickly. I slammed my laptop shut with a violence that echoed in the quiet room. I began to pace the length of my living room, my movements jerky and agitated. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. Every muscle in my body was coiled, vibrating with a restless energy that refused to settle. "No," I whispered, the sound raspy and brittle. "This isn't right." The air in the room felt thick, charged with an unbearable tension that made my vision swim slightly at the edges.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I felt a sudden, alarming drop in temperature. My hands started trembling, a fine, uncontrollable tremor that betrayed the internal upheaval. I leaned over the desk, my breath hitching as I read the publication details. The realization hit me with the force of a physical impact, and a deep, churning sensation rose in my throat. I pushed back from the desk, stumbling a step. I began to pace the narrow hallway, my gait quick and sharp, my eyes darting around the room as if searching for an escape route. A low growl escaped my chest, a sound raw and involuntary. I stopped by the window, gripping the sill until my knuckles were white, the sheer injustice of it pressing down on me until I felt physically unable to breathe evenly.
